Hartford Insurance Company Of The Midwests Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File A Complaint-In-Intervention; Declaration Of Jenna L. Fischer-Reyes In Support
Off Calendar Mar-27-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Zachariah D. Hansen, Esq., The Arns Law Firm. 415-495-7800, appearing for Plaintiff;
Jenna L. Fischer, Esq., Law Officecs of Johnn A. Hauser, 714-371-2739, appearing for Lien Claimant, Hartford Insuarnace Company of the Midwest, (CourtCall)

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

INTERVENOR HARTFORD INSURANCE COMPANY OF THE MIDWEST'S Motion For Leave To File A Complaint-In-Intervention.

Complainant-in-Intervention Harford Insurance Company of the Midwest's motion for leave to file a complaint-in-intervention is granted. An employer's workers' compensation insurer has an unconditional statutory right to intervene in an action brought by the employee against a third-party tortfeasor "at any time before trial on the facts." (Lab. Code 3853; Bailey v. Reliance Ins. Co. (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 449, 454.) Plaintiff did not file timely opposition to the motion. In any event, an insurer's motion for leave to intervene in an employee's third-party action is properly denied only when it is filed after the action has been settled and dismissed. (Aetna Cas. & Sur. Co. v. Superior Court (1993) 20 Cal.App.4th 1502, 1506, 1509 [where insurer's motion for allowance of workers' compensation lien against the proceeds of the settlement was filed "[w]eeks after the complaint was dismissed," it was untimely]; see also Mar v. Sakti Internat. Corp. (1992) 9 Cal.App.4th 1780, 1785 [employer that sought to intervene in injured employee's suit against third parties prior to scheduled trial date has as a matter of law timely applied to intervene, even though employee and defendants had settled].) Here, Hartford's motion was filed on March 4, 2019, before Plaintiff filed its request for dismissal on March 21. (Cf. Andersen v. Barton Memorial Hospital, Inc. (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 678, 685 n.9 ["'Intervention cannot take place after an action has been dismissed.'"].)

Order is signed

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Webcor Obayashi Joint Ventures
Off Calendar Dec-04-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Zacariah D. Hansen, Esq. 415-495-7800 of the Arns Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Jonathan Davis, Esq. 415-495-7800 of the Arns Law Firm for the Plaintiff through Court Call.
Richard Ames, Esq. 415-986-5900 of Gordon Rees LLP for Defendant SKANSKA USA BUILDING, INC., WEBCOR / OBAYASHI.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court admonishes Defendant's Counsel re: motions and courtesy copies. Defendant argues. The Court looks at the 'mccasky" case. The Court overrules the objection to Mr. Garcia's testimony on page 30 of the Deposition. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT WEBCOR / OBAYASHI JOINT VENTURE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Webcor/Obayashi Joint Venture's motion for summary judgment is denied. There is a triable dispute whether WOJV negligently retained control of the worksite and affirmatively contributed to Mr. Hernandez's injury. Plaintiffs presented evidence showing that WOJV retained control over the worksite (Ex. 6, Burke Depo., 54:20-25; 9:5-12) and made the decision to send Anvil into the injury zone even though the zone had yet to be made safe by Skanska or properly turned over to WOJV. (Ex. 4, Spillane Depo., 30:2-12; Ex. 5, Garcia Depo., 30:1-7.) "While the passive permitting of an unsafe condition to occur is not an affirmative contribution, the act of directing that it occur is active participation." (Tverberg v. Fillner Construction (2012) 202 CalApp.4th 1439, 1448.)

Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312 which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and include overruling the evidentiary objection to Mr. Garcia on page 30 of the Deposition. Defendant submits a proposed order on evidentiary objections. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Dec-04-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Zacariah D. Hansen, Esq. 415-495-7800 of the Arns Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Jonathan Davis, Esq. 415-495-7800 of the Arns Law Firm for the Plaintiff through Court Call.
Richard Ames, Esq. 415-986-5900 of Gordon Rees LLP for Defendant SKANSKA USA BUILDING, INC., WEBCOR / OBAYASHI.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court admonishes Defendant's Counsel re: motions and courtesy copies. Defendant argues. The Court looks at the 'mccasky" case. The Court overrules the objection to Mr. Garcia's testimony on page 30 of the Deposition. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT SKANSKA USA BUILDING, INC., WEBCOR / OBAYASHI JOINT VENTURE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the Tentative Ruling. Defendant Skanska USA Building, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Skanska fails to maintain its initial burden of production per CCP section 437c. The Privette doctrine does not apply to a non-hirer subcontractor such as Skanska in this case. The doctrine is based on notions of fairness. "[A]n independent contractor's employee should not be allowed to recover damages from the contractor's hirer, who 'is indirectly paying for the cost of [workers' compensation] coverage, which the [hired] contractor presumably has calculated into the contract price.'" (Tverberg v. Fillner Construction, Inc. (2010) 49 Cal.4th 518, 525.) No such fairness issue exists with regard to a non-hirer such as Skanska. Alvarez v. Seaside Transportation Services LLC (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 635 does not extend the Privette doctrine to all subcontractors on a worksite. The case does not expressly address that issue and it appears that the subcontractors in that case obtained summary judgment because they were being sued as agents of the hirer. (Alvarez, 13 Cal.App.5th at 644, fn.4.) In this case, plaintiffs are suing Skanska for its direct negligence. Paragraphs 17-21 of the complaint allege that Skanska breached its duty to maintain and control the area where the accident occurred. Skanska does not present evidence showing that (1) it met that duty and (2) plaintiffs' claim cannot be established. As an additional ground to deny the motion, even if Skanska had satisfied its initial summary motion burden, plaintiffs created a triable dispute by presenting evidence showing that Skanska did in fact breach its duty. That evidence included Skanska had control of the zone where the injury occurred and a responsibility to put warning barriers up. (Ex. 4, Spillane Depo., 23:13-24:1; 39:19-25; 59:11-15; 89:23-90:21 (Webcor's PMK); Ex. 6 Burke Depo., 45:5-7; 53:12-17; 64:21-23; 66:24-67:19; 69:8-16 (Webcor's Construction Manager).) There is also evidence that Skanska had a responsibility to abate hazards within its controlled zone. (Ex. 10, Buckignani Depo., 110:6-14 (Webcor Senior Safety Manager).) Skanska does not establish that it delineated the zone or abated the hazards.

Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312 which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and include overruling the evidentiary objection to Mr. Garcia on page 30 of the Deposition. Defendant submits a proposed order on evidentiary objections. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)
